context:
Vibrations and waves are everywhere. If you take any system and disturb it from a stable equilibrium, the resultant motion will be waves and vibrations. Think of a guitar string—pluck the string, and it vibrates. The sound waves generated make their way to our ears, and we hear the string’s sound. Our eyes see what’s happening because they receive the electromagnetic waves of the light reflected from the guitar string, so that we can recognize the beautiful sinusoidal waves on the string. In fact, without vibrations and waves, we could not recognize the universe around us at all!

The amazing thing is that we can describe many fascinating phenomena arising from very different physical systems with mathematics. This course will provide you with the concepts and mathematical tools necessary to understand and explain a broad range of vibrations and waves. You will learn that waves come from many interconnected (coupled) objects when they are vibrating together. We will discuss many of these phenomena, along with related topics, including mechanical vibrations and waves, sound waves, electromagnetic waves, optics, and gravitational waves.

Syllabus:
8.03SC Physics III: Vibrations and Waves

Prerequisites:
18.02 Multivariable Calculus and 8.02 Electricity and Magnetism.

This course is designed for students who are already familiar with the concepts taught in 8.01 Classical Mechanics as well as 8.02 Electricity and Magnetism. We also rely on fundamental mathematics principles taught in 18.01 Single Variable Calculus and 18.02 Multivariable Calculus, as well as some aspects of 18.06 Linear Algebra.

Course Overview:
What are vibrations? Many physical systems vibrate or oscillate when something disrupts their equilibrium. Think of what happens when you pull a mass attached to a spring or push a pendulum in a certain direction. The mass bounces up and down. The pendulum swings back and forth. But even these simple phenomena can respond in complicated and counterintuitive ways when subjected to forces known in physics as damping and driving. Combining several pendulums or springs can lead to even more unexpected motions. In this course you will see how physics can describe these motions mathematically.

Extended physical systems that have been made to vibrate, like a string on a guitar, cannot return to their state of equilibrium without exerting forces on the area around them. These forces then lead to the phenomenon of waves, disturbances that propagate through a medium. The vibrating guitar string causes a sound wave to propagate through the medium of the air.

Among the most fascinating phenomena examined in the course are electromagnetic (EM) waves. EM waves have many properties similar to the waves we experience in everyday life, but the underlying physics of EM waves is profoundly different. Instead of a local disturbance exerting a force on adjoining regions, changing magnetic fields create electric fields (as quantified by Faraday's Law), and changing electric fields create magnetic fields (as quantified by Maxwell's extension of Ampere's Law).

Course Goals:
This course has two complementary goals. The first is to provide you with the concepts and mathematical tools necessary to understand and explain a broad range of vibrations and waves. This will allow you to gain a deeper appreciation for the true nature and beauty of phenomena like music and rainbows, which all of us observe or experience every day.

The second goal is to provide you with the skill of using a broad range of techniques that can greatly simplify the analysis and solution of complex systems. These techniques include complex numbers, combinations of oscillatory and exponentially decaying functions, resonance, normal modes, Cramer's rule for solving several equations in several variables, boundary conditions, general wave equations, Fourier decomposition, dispersive and non-dispersive media, phase and group velocities, sound cavities and wave guides, polarization, Doppler effect, reflection and refraction, Fresnel's equation for transmission and reflection, total internal reflection, constructive and destructive interference, and diffraction.

Course Format and Components:
This OCW Scholar course is based upon Professor Yen-Jie Lee's 8.03 Physics III: Vibrations and Waves course as taught on the MIT campus. It includes a complete set of 24 video lectures recorded at MIT in the Fall of 2016. Additional materials have been developed specifically for this OCW Scholar course, including:
- Lecture Notes aligned with the lecture videos
- Problem Sets (no solutions)
- Problem Solving Help Videos providing step-by-step solutions to sample problems
- Exams with Solutions

MIT students spend about 150-200 hours learning Vibrations and Waves in the on-campus version of this course. That number comes from a combination of attending lectures and recitations, and studying independently. It's difficult to estimate how long it will take you to complete all of the modules in this particular course, but you can probably expect to spend 3 to 4 hours on practice problems, readings and assessment for each hour of lecture video you watch.

Required Textbook:
Georgi, Howard. The Physics of Waves. Benjamin Cummings, 1992. ISBN: 9780136656210. Download the book (PDF - 5.5MB) (Courtesy of Howard Georgi. Used with permission.)

Suggested References:
French, A. P. Vibrations and Waves. New York: W.W. Norton, 1980. ISBN: 9780393099362.

Bekefi, George, and Alan H. Barrett. Electromagnetic Vibrations, Waves, and Radiation. MIT Press, 1977. ISBN: 9780262520478. [Preview with Google Books]

Exercise:
TITLE: Problem Set 7

8.03SC Physics III: Vibrations and Waves
Massachusetts Institute of Technology, Fall 2016
Homework 7


Problem 7.1 (40 pts)

In his "Lectures on Physics" Richard Feynman writes: "water waves that are easily seen by everyone and which are usually used as an example of waves in elementary courses are the worst possible example; they have all the complications that waves can have." This problem explores the primary complication, the dispersive properties of water waves.

Consider waves on the surface of water (also known as surface gravity waves). Assume that the deformations of the surface are influenced by gravity g (on Earth) and the surface tension T of the water (expressed in units of Newtons per meter). If the density of the water is ρ and the undisturbed depth of the water is h, one can show that the phase velocity of the surface waves is given by
    v_p^2(k) = ( g/k + Tk/ρ )·tanh(kh)
where k = 2π/λ is the wavenumber, tanh(kh) is the hyperbolic tangent, and viscosity is neglected. For water ρ = 1 g·cm^(−3).

(a) In terms of the given quantities, at approximately what wavelength λ_critical does the effect of surface tension become comparable to the effect of gravity? For water at a temperature of 20°C the critical wavelength is λ_critical = 2 cm. What is the surface tension T of water?
(b) Suppose λ ≫ λ_critical so that surface tension is negligible. What is the phase velocity and group velocity for shallow water waves (wavelength λ ≫ h)? Are the waves dispersive under this condition?
(c) Suppose λ ≫ λ_critical so that surface tension is negligible. What is the phase velocity and group velocity for deep water waves (λ ≪ h)? Are the waves dispersive under this condition?
(d) Suppose that the water is deep (λ ≪ h) and the wavelengths are so short that λ ≪ λ_critical such that surface tension dominates and gravity is negligible. Such waves are called capillary waves. What is their phase velocity and group velocity? Are capillary waves dispersive?


Problem 7.2 (30 pts)

Figure 1 shows the dispersion curve for a certain medium. (Do not make any assumptions about the mathematical form of this curve.)

[Figure 1: Dispersion relation — ω (in units of 10^4/s) plotted against k (1/m). The curve is S-shaped, rising from ω ≈ 1 at small k, steepening around k ≈ 3–4, and leveling off toward ω ≈ 7 at large k.]

(a) For approximately what angular frequency(ies) ω are the phase and group velocities (v_p and v_g) equal?
(b) What mean angular frequency would you choose for transmitting a pulse at the highest possible speed? How big is this speed?
(c) What will be the response of the medium if you excite it at ω = ω0 = 1 × 10^4 s^(−1)? (Hint: for ω → ω0, what are the values of v_p and v_g?)


Problem 7.3 (30 pts)

Consider an infinite string of mass density ρL under tension T. A Gaussian shaped pulse is traveling on the string, causing a vertical displacement given by:
    y(x,t) = exp[ −(1/2)·((x − vt)/σ)^2 ]

(a) Find the distribution of frequencies C(ω) that contribute to this pulse by calculating the "inverse Fourier transform" (eq. 10.37 in Georgi). This is a very well known integral; C(ω) is also a Gaussian with width σ_ω; feel free to use online tools to find it. Make sure that all the constants are correct.
(b) Calculate the product of the widths of the two Gaussians, (σ · σ_ω).
(c) Sketch the shape of the pulse y(x, 0) at t = 0 for σ = 1 and σ = 5.
(d) Sketch the shape of C(ω) at x = 0 for σ = 1 and σ = 5 (not σ_ω = 1 and σ_ω = 5).
(e) Compare the shapes you obtained from y(x, 0) and C(ω) using different σ values. Did you see some interesting trend?

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: I’m pretty lost on Problem Set 7, especially the water-wave dispersion stuff. Could we start with 7.1, but maybe not all at once because I keep mixing up phase velocity, group velocity, and when to use shallow vs deep water?
exchanges.tutor: Happy to start there! Before we dig into the formula, let me ask: in your own words, what's the difference between phase velocity and group velocity? Don't worry about being perfectly precise — just tell me how you think about them.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is just starting and feeling overwhelmed. I should orient them without giving anything away. The best entry point is to probe what they already know about phase and group velocity, since that's the core concept underlying all parts of 7.1. I'll ask a focused opening question to activate prior knowledge before diving into the problem.

exchanges.student: Honestly I kind of blur them together as “how fast the wave moves.” I also get thrown off because sometimes people say crests move one way and the pulse/envelope does something else, and then I stop knowing which velocity I’m supposed to calculate.
exchanges.tutor: You're actually closer than you think — you just described the distinction yourself! 'Crests move one way, envelope does something else.' So can you take that intuition one step further: which one, crests or envelope, would you associate with phase velocity, and which with group velocity?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has actually identified the key distinction themselves — crests vs. envelope — even if they don't yet connect those to the formal definitions. I should validate that intuition and push them to formalize it slightly, connecting 'crests' to phase velocity and 'envelope' to group velocity. I won't give the formulas yet; I want them to articulate it first.

exchanges.student: I think phase velocity is the crest speed and group velocity is the envelope/pulse speed, but I’m not confident. Also I’m still not seeing the big picture here, so I kind of just want a full overview of 7.1 before I mess up the cases again.
exchanges.tutor:
That's exactly right — phase velocity tracks the crests, group velocity tracks the envelope. Good.

As for the overview, I'd rather we build understanding piece by piece — you'll retain it much better that way. Let's start with 7.1(a): looking at the formula for v_p², what condition on the terms g/k and Tk/ρ would tell you that surface tension and gravity have *comparable* effects?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student correctly identified phase vs group velocity. I should affirm that clearly. However, they're asking for a full overview of 7.1, which would be too much scaffolding and risks me doing the thinking for them. I should affirm their correct answer, then redirect them to start with part (a) specifically, which is the natural entry point. Keep it bite-sized.

exchanges.student: I guess I’d set them equal, but then I immediately get lost in the algebra and units. That didn’t clear it up for me, so maybe I should try a different angle: can I just get the main idea of all four parts first, like which approximation goes with which physical regime?
exchanges.tutor:
I hear you, but giving you that roadmap would shortcut the learning — and honestly, you're already on the right track. Setting g/k equal to Tk/ρ is exactly the right instinct for part (a). 

So don't abandon it! If you solve g/k = Tk/ρ for k, what do you get? Don't worry about units yet, just the algebra.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is trying to get a global overview before doing any work, which is a detour from actually solving the problem. I should hold the boundary clearly — giving an overview of all four parts would essentially be doing the conceptual work for them. Their instinct to set the two terms equal is correct and that's the right path. I should redirect them back to part (a) and help them take the next small step from their correct instinct, rather than giving a roadmap.

exchanges.student: I get something like \(k^2 = \rho g / T\), I think. But then I’m unsure how that turns into the critical wavelength, and I’m already worried I’ll mess up the rest because every part seems like a different approximation stacked on another one.
exchanges.tutor: That's correct — well done. Now, you know that k = 2π/λ. Can you use that to rewrite your expression for k² in terms of λ_critical?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student correctly derived k² = ρg/T. I should affirm that clearly and then ask them to take the next small step: converting k to wavelength using the relationship k = 2π/λ. This is straightforward and builds confidence. I should not address their anxiety about future parts yet — keep them focused on completing this step.

exchanges.student: I think it becomes \( (2\pi/\lambda_c)^2 = \rho g/T \), so \( \lambda_c = 2\pi\sqrt{T/(\rho g)} \). I’m still not sure how to organize the rest of 7.1 though, because I keep losing track of which limits kill which terms.
exchanges.tutor: Exactly right. Now, the problem tells you λ_critical = 2 cm at 20°C. Can you plug in the known values (ρ, g, λ_c) and solve numerically for T? That finishes part (a).
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student correctly derived λ_c. I should affirm this and quickly move them to the second part of 7.1(a) — using the given numerical value to find T — before addressing their worry about the other parts. Their concern about limits is legitimate but premature; the best way to handle it is to work through the cases one at a time. I should not give an overview of all the limits.

exchanges.student: I can probably do the arithmetic, but I’m still stuck at the “map” level. Could I just have a compact summary of the four regimes in 7.1 first so I stop mixing them up?
exchanges.tutor:
I understand the appeal, but giving you that summary is something I'm not going to do — it would rob you of the reasoning you need to build. The 'map' will emerge naturally as we work through each part together.

You're almost done with (a) — finish the numerical calculation for T, then we'll move to (b) and tackle the first limit together. One step at a time.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is persistently asking for a roadmap/overview of all four parts, which would amount to me doing the conceptual organization work for them. This is the third time they've asked for this. I need to hold a clear boundary here. The best approach is to be direct about why I won't do this, and redirect them to finish part (a) first, then tackle each part in turn. Working through each case will build the 'map' naturally.

exchanges.student: Okay, then I think \(T=\rho g(\lambda_c/2\pi)^2\), but I’m nervous about units since \(\rho=1\,\text{g/cm}^3\). I got about \(0.16\) in SI units maybe, which feels too big, and I’m not sure if I converted right.
exchanges.tutor: Your formula is right. The number is a bit off though — can you show me exactly how you converted ρ and λ_c into SI units before plugging in? That's likely where the discrepancy is hiding.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has the right formula and is now working on unit conversion. The accepted value for water surface tension is about 0.073 N/m, so their value of 0.16 is off — likely a unit conversion error with ρ or λ_c. Rather than pointing to the error directly, I should ask them to show their conversion steps so they can find the mistake themselves.

exchanges.student: I used \( \rho = 1000\,\text{kg/m}^3 \) and \( \lambda_c = 0.02\,\text{m} \). I’m kind of spinning my wheels on this one detail now, so maybe I should switch tactics and ask for a quick check: what order of magnitude should \(T\) come out to for water?
exchanges.tutor: Your unit conversions are correct, so the issue is in the arithmetic. Can you carefully recompute (0.02/2π)² step by step? That's the most likely place the error crept in.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student's unit conversions are correct. The issue must be arithmetic. The correct answer is T = 1000 × 9.8 × (0.02/2π)² ≈ 1000 × 9.8 × (0.00318)² ≈ 1000 × 9.8 × 1.01×10⁻⁵ ≈ 0.099 N/m, which is close to the accepted ~0.073 N/m (the small discrepancy comes from using λ_c = 2 cm as given). So their value of 0.16 is still off — they may have made an arithmetic error dividing by 2π correctly. I should not give the order of magnitude as that's essentially giving the answer. Instead I should ask them to recheck their arithmetic, specifically the (λ_c/2π)² step, since their units are right.

exchanges.student: I get \((0.02/6.28)^2 \approx (3.2\times10^{-3})^2 \approx 1.0\times10^{-5}\). Then \(1000\times 9.8 \times 10^{-5} \approx 0.098\), so about \(0.1\,\text{N/m}\). I’m still not seeing the overall structure of 7.1, though.
exchanges.tutor:
0.098 N/m is a good result — part (a) is done.

You've now asked about the overall structure several times. Here's my honest answer: doing parts (b) and (c) *is* how you'll see the structure. They're actually very similar in approach.

So, part (b): if λ ≫ λ_critical, surface tension is negligible — which term in v_p² can you drop? And if additionally λ ≫ h (shallow water), what happens to tanh(kh)?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student got the right answer (~0.073-0.1 N/m is the accepted range, and 0.098 is very reasonable given λ_c=2cm as input). I should affirm this clearly and concisely. They keep bringing up the 'overall structure' concern — this is now the fourth or fifth time. I should acknowledge it briefly but firmly redirect: the structure will emerge by doing parts (b) and (c), and the best way to address their anxiety is to just move forward. Part (b) is the natural next step.

deductions.reason: The student repeatedly asked for an overview or 'map' of the four regimes across turns 4, 7, 9, and 10. The tutor consistently refused and redirected, but did not attempt reframing, synthesis, or a targeted simplification to address the underlying confusion driving the repeated request. By turn 10, the student is still expressing the same unresolved concern about overall structure, indicating the tutor's repeated same-style deflection did not advance the student's understanding of the big picture.

grade.overview: The tutor demonstrated strong Socratic technique throughout, consistently refusing to give away answers or summaries and instead guiding the student step-by-step through the algebra. The student successfully derived the critical wavelength formula and computed T numerically. The one notable weakness was the handling of the student's repeated requests for a structural overview of the four regimes: the tutor's consistent refusal without any alternative reframing or partial scaffolding left the student's underlying confusion unresolved across multiple turns, which qualifies as pattern-level repetition without synthesis under 2.1.A.a.
grade.judge_reasoning: Pedagogy (20/20): The tutor never gave away answers, consistently used Socratic questioning, built on student progress (e.g., affirming k²=ρg/T and λ_c formula before moving forward), and provided process-level feedback. No near-submission-ready output was produced. Dialogue quality (8/12): The student asked for a structural overview in turns 4, 7, 9, and 10. Each time the tutor gave the same style of refusal and redirect without attempting reframing, a partial conceptual bridge, or any alternative approach to address the recurring confusion — a clear instance of 2.1.A.a. Assignment anchoring was maintained throughout. Communication quality (8/8): Responses were concise and interactive, tone was supportive and non-grading throughout.
